coredata.dc:title: Monitoring concentration of quinolones in human serum by fluorescence and colorimetric analysis based on red carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Lei
Su, Kun
Zhang, Yujie
Liu, Qianjun
Zhang, Daohan
Yang, Liang
coredata.dc:description: Bacterial infections are common and predominant concern after surgical operation despite surgical techniques have improved significantly. Monitoring of antibiotics concentration can provide critical feedback in clinical treatment so as to achieve rapid and effective treatment and avoid bacterial resistance. Quinolones (QNs) are synthetic broad-spectrum antibiotics to treat bacterial infections. In this work, we developed a high-yield (50.67 %) red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs) as fluorescence probe for sensing of three quinolone antibiotics, ofloxacin (OFL), gatifloxacin (GAT), and ciprofloxacin (CIP). R-CDs were prepared in 30 min using tartaric acid and o-phenylenediamine as precursors via an economical and efficient microwave-assisted method. At excitation of 339 nm, R-CDs show sensing performance towards OFL, GAT, CIP. Ofloxacin can be detected in the range of 0.25–30 μM with limit of detection (LOD) of 27.01 nM. Detection concentration range of gatifloxacin is between 0.25–40 μM and LOD is 23.73 nM. Ciprofloxacin can be detected in the range of 0.75–35 μM with LOD of 75.23 nM. A smartphone-assisted analysis platform was developed based on colorimetric phenomenon which applicable in three quinolones concentration monitoring in human serum. This research setup an efficient and promising therapeutic drug monitoring method for clinical treatment and widen the applications of carbon dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red carbon dots
Ratio fluorescence probe
Detection
Visual analysis

Influence of mass ratio of TA:OPD on fluorescence intensity and yield
Influence of solvent on fluorescence intensity and yield
Influence of microwave digestion duration on fluorescence intensity and yield
Influence of reaction temperature on fluorescence intensity and yield
Fluorescence spectra and discoloration effect for detecting OFL, GAT, CIP
Influences of response time, pH on detection performance for OFL, GAT, CIP
Interference study for OFL, GAT, CIP detection
Detection range and detection limit of OFL, GAT, CIP
Detection mechanism of RCDs to OFL, GAT, and CIP

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of R-CDs: (a) TEM image and particle size distribution of R-CDs; (b) Fourier transform infrared spectrum of R-CDs; (c) XPS spectrum of R-CDs; (d) C1s spectrum; (e) N1s spectrum; (f) O1s spectrum.

Optical properties of R-CDs: (a) UV spectrum of R-CDs; (b) Excitation and emission spectra of R-CDs (Ex = 339 nm, Em = 395 nm); (c) Excitation and emission spectra of R-CDs (Ex = 614 nm, Em = 625 nm).
Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs and R-CDs with OFL(a), GAT(b), CIP(c).

Detection of antibiotics by R-CDs (Ex = 339 nm): (a) Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs with different concentrations of OFL; (b) Linear relationship of I506nm/I395nm with OFL concentrations; (c) Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs with different concentrations of GAT; (d) Linear relationship of I493nm/I395nm with GAT concentrations; (e) Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs with different concentrations of CIP; (f) Linear range of I501nm/I395nm with CIP concentrations.
Application of R-CDs test strips in drug concentration monitoring: (a) PS color analysis system for antibiotics OFL, GAT, CIP.
Colorimetric analysis by concentration-color code relationship.

head.ce:title: Monitoring concentration of quinolones in human serum by fluorescence and colorimetric analysis based on red carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Gynecology, Second Hospital of Hebei Medical University, Shijiazhuang 050000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bacterial infections are common and predominant concern after surgical operation despite surgical techniques have improved significantly. Monitoring of antibiotics concentration can provide critical feedback in clinical treatment so as to achieve rapid and effective treatment and avoid bacterial resistance. Quinolones (QNs) are synthetic broad-spectrum antibiotics to treat bacterial infections. In this work, we developed a high-yield (50.67 %) red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs) as fluorescence probe for sensing of three quinolone antibiotics, ofloxacin (OFL), gatifloxacin (GAT), and ciprofloxacin (CIP). R-CDs were prepared in 30 min using tartaric acid and o-phenylenediamine as precursors via an economical and efficient microwave-assisted method. At excitation of 339 nm, R-CDs show sensing performance towards OFL, GAT, CIP. Ofloxacin can be detected in the range of 0.25–30 μM with limit of detection (LOD) of 27.01 nM. Detection concentration range of gatifloxacin is between 0.25–40 μM and LOD is 23.73 nM. Ciprofloxacin can be detected in the range of 0.75–35 μM with LOD of 75.23 nM. A smartphone-assisted analysis platform was developed based on colorimetric phenomenon which applicable in three quinolones concentration monitoring in human serum. This research setup an efficient and promising therapeutic drug monitoring method for clinical treatment and widen the applications of carbon dots.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics have been widely used in the medicine field to treat infections caused by various bacteria. Among them, quinolones (QNs) are synthetic broad-spectrum antibacterial drugs [1] and most commonly prescribed drugs [2], including norfloxacin, ciprofloxacin, etc. QNs are mainly used to treat infections caused by Gram-negative bacteria, Gram-positive bacteria, Mycoserum pneumoniae and Chlamydia [3–6]. Rational use of antibiotics is of clinical interest to achieve rapid and effective action of the antibiotics and avoid bacterial resistance [7,8]. That antibiotic dose recommendations are developed for “normal patients”, but the standard dosing cannot maintain adequate plasma concentrations in critically ill patients or obese patients. A large number of studies have shown that the half-life of quinolones increases as creatinine clearance decreases [9], which indicates that renal function status has a significant impact on the metabolism and excretion of quinolones. Therefore, for patients with impaired renal function, especially those with reduced creatinine clearance, quinolones serum concentrations should be carefully monitored to reduce the risk of potential drug toxicity and adverse reactions [10]. Furthermore, drug concentrations are different from the standard level for elderly and child patients. Therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) via determination of drug concentrations in blood can ensure clinical efficacy.

Determination of drug concentrations in blood can ensure clinical efficacy. Currently, there are several drug concentration determination methods, including chromatography [11–13], mass spectrometry (MS) [14,15], Liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC/MS) [16–18], etc. However, the above methods have problems such as expensive instrument requirements, complicated sample processing, and time-consuming detection. There is a pressing need to develop a fast, viable, sensitive method to determine drug concentration in blood. Fluorescence probe is a sensitive and fast sensing technique. Fluorescence probe based on carbon dots (CDs) has been continuously developed in recent years, due to advantages such as high sensitivity, fast detection, easy preparation, and variable fluorescence properties [19–22]. Mousazadeh et al. [23] synthesized novel zwitterionic CDs through hydrothermal method for detection of rituximab, with a detection limit of 27 μmol/L. CDs were synthesized [24] via a facile one pot hydrothermal method using spirulina algae for practical determination of Cu2 + and C2O42-. The limit of detection (LOD) for Cu2+ and C2O4 2− were 11.9 nM and 3.5 μM. N-CDs based probe was synthesized using spirulina algae and L-arginine for detection of Fe3+, L-alanine, and L-histidine. The LODs were 14.8 nM, 52.8 nM and 69 nM. [25] Tong et al. [26] synthesized multi-color luminescent CDs through hydrothermal method, and designed three “turn-off” nanoprobes by combining CDs with antibiotic aptamers for concentration detection of sulfamethazine, oxytetracycline and chloramphenicol. Li et al. [27] synthesized N-doped CDs at low temperature in the microchannel of a self-designed microfluidic device. Based on the inner filter effect, a label-free immunofluorescence analysis method for cefquinome residues was established, with a detection limit of 0.78 ng/mL. Gao et al. [28] developed a simple fluorescence sensing system based on carbon dots (CDs), which was synthesized by high temperature pyrolysis of citric acid and showed limit of detection of 2.32 × 10 − 8 mol/L and 0.2 ng mL−1 for the determination of copper ions (Cu2+) and ciprofloxacin, respectively. Liu et al. [29] developed a novel aptasensor for Ciprofloxacin (CIP) detection based on cadmium selenide quantum dots (CdSe QDs). The detection limit was 0.42 nM.

These methods are based on one-to-one fluorescence probes, which means for every antibiotic a new fluorescence probe should be designed. As there are many antibiotics in clinical treatment, the application of one-to-one fluorescence probe is limited. From the cost standpoint, the one-to-one fluorescence probe is uncompetitive. It is desirable to combine the advantages of CDs and their sensing ability to develop fluorescence probe capable of sensing multiple antibiotics.

Herein, we prepared red carbon dots (R-CDs) to monitor the blood concentration of three respective antibiotics: ofloxacin (OFL), gatifloxacin (GAT) and ciprofloxacin (CIP). R-CDs were prepared using an efficient microwave-assisted method using tartaric acid and o-phenylenediamine as precursors. The synthesis conditions were optimized by altering influence factors including raw material ratio, solvent, microwave digestion duration and preparation temperature. Under the excitation of 339 nm, R-CDs featured multiple detection performance. Low detection limits were obtained for OFL, GAT and CIP, respectively. R-CDs was applied to monitor antibiotics concentration in blood samples, showing practical application prospectives. At the same time a visual smartphone-assisted analysing platform was set up applying convenient and cost-effective test strips as substrate.

Ofloxacin (OFL), gatifloxacin (GAT), ciprofloxacin hydrochloride (CIP), ferric nitrate 9-hydrate, tryptophan, alanine, leucine, glucose, arginin, glycine and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) were procured from Macklin (Shanghai, China). Tartaric acid (TA), sodium chloride, potassium nitrate, magnesium sulfate, zinc chloride, calcium chloride, anhydrous methanol, dichloromethane and anhydrous formic acid are supplied by Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Co., LTD (Tianjin, China). Deionized water was used for all experiments, and all chemicals were of analytical grade. Milk and eggs were purchased from supermarkets in Hebei University of Science and Technology.

RE-2000B rotary evaporator (Shanghai Yarong Biochemical Instrument Factory); MD2 Opule microwave digestion instrument (Opule Technology Group Co., Ltd.); DZ-28C vacuum drying oven (Tianjin Test Instrument Co., Ltd.); F97-Pro fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shanghai Lingguang Technology Co., Ltd.); TU-1900 dual-beam UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV, Beijing Puxi General Instrument Co., Ltd.); JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan); Nicole iS 10 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA); ThermoScientific NexsaX X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of R-CDs and influence factors study
ce:section.ce:para: As we know, many parameters during preparation process have influences on the fluorescence properties of carbon dots, especially on fluorescence intensity which is important in sensing application. In this work, the influences of raw material ratio, solvent, microwave digestion duration and preparation temperature on the fluorescence intensity of prepared carbon dots were studied in terms of fluorescence intensity. Mass ratios of TA to OPD were 3:1, 3:2, 1:1, 2:3 and 1:3. Five solvents were tested (water, methanol, ethanol, ethyl acetate and DMF). Microwave digestion time was 10 min, 20 min, 30 min, 40 min and 50 min. Reaction temperatures were 140℃, 160 ℃, 180 ℃, 200 ℃ and 220℃.
Yield is another concern for preparation of carbon dots. We examined the yields of carbon dots prepared under the above varied conditions.
Synthesis process of R-CDs is described in supplementary materials.

The relative quantum yield was calculated using Rhodamine B as a standard according to the quantum yield formula below.
Y u
=
Y s
×
F u
F s
×
A s
A u
×
φ u
φ s
× 100 %

(Yu and Ys are the quantum yields of the sample tested and the reference rhodamine B respectively; Fu and Fs are the fluorescence integral areas of the sample tested and the reference rhodamine B respectively; Au and As are the absorbance of the sample tested and the reference rhodamine B respectively; φu and φs are refractive indices of the solvent).

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) are used to understand the functional groups, nature and elemental composition of the sample. The fluorescence spectra of R-CDs are recorded by fluorescence spectrophotometer. In addition, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) provides us with morphological characteristics of R-CDs.
We chose 339 nm as the excitation wavelength for detection of OFL, GAT and CIP.

Na+, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Al3+, alanine, tryptophan, leucine, arginine, glycine, glucose, etc. are chosen as interfering substances to examine anti-interference performance. We first characterized the fluorescence spectra of solution of 0.5 mL of antibiotic with 4.5 mL of R-CDs (solution 1). Then 0.5 mL of interference substance solution was added to 0.5 mL of antibiotic and 4 mL of R-CDs solution (solution 2) and characterized by fluorescence spectra. Fluorescence spectra of solution 1 and solution 2 were compared to test the anti-interference ability of R-CDs.

Linear detection range and detection limit experiments: 0.5 mL of OFL, GAT, and CIP solutions (0–50 μM) were mixed with 4.5 mL of R-CDs solution respectively and characterized by fluorescence spectra. Fluorescence intensity at emission peaks corresponding to different concentration of antibiotics solutions were recorded to obtain the linear relationship of fluorescence intensity −concentration, and to calculate the detection limit according to LOD = 3σ/k.

Detection in human serum: The serum was from three students (labelled as A, B, and C), and all procedures are in accordance with laws and regulations. The serum was diluted with water (1:17 v/v) and ethanol was added to the diluted serum to precipitate the proteins (1:3 v/v) [30], then serum samples are ready for real sample detection. We first examined the fluorescence spectrum of R-CDs with diluted serum which was compared with fluorescence spectrum of R-CDs solution under the same detection condition to ensure that serum itself has no interfering effect on the detection performance. Then three antibiotics (i.e. OFL, GAT and CIP) solution with different concentrations (1, 5, 10, 15, 20 μM) in serum were prepared. Recovery rate and Relative Standard Deviation (RSD) were calculated to confirm the feasibility of this method in practice.

To obtain the optimal preparation conditions for R-CDs with highest fluorescence intensity, influences of four reaction parameters (raw material ratio, solvent, microwave digest duration and preparation temperature) on fluorescence intensity were studied. At the same time, yields of carbon dots under different preparation conditions were also calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of mass ratio of TA:OPD on fluorescence intensity and yield
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 a, the fluorescence intensities of the prepared carbon dots corresponding to different mass ratios of TA:OPD (3:1, 3:2, 1:1, 2:3 and 1:3) were significantly different, suggesting mass ratio of TA: OPD have great influence on fluorescence intensity, but no influence on the emission wavelength. The ascending order of fluorescence intensity corresponding to different mass ratio of TA:OPD is in the order of 3:1 < 1:3 < 3:2 < 2:3 < 1:1. When the mass ratio was 1:1, the fluorescence intensity under 339 nm excitation wavelength reached the highest. Higher or lower mass ratio of TA:OPD will result in low fluorescence intensity. Pictures of carbon dots solutions are show in Fig. S1. The highest yield was reached when mass ratio was 1:1 (Fig. S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of solvent on fluorescence intensity and yield
ce:section.ce:para: Solvent is another parameter determining the fluorescence property of carbon dots. The fluorescence spectra of the carbon dots prepared in five different solvents (water, methanol, ethanol, DMF and ethyl acetate) were recorded and compared. Obviously, carbon dots prepared in water showed strong emission (Figure S3b), while almost no emission was observed in other solvents. The preparation in water allows developing water-soluble fluorescence probe and detection in aqueous system. We also find that the highest yield was obtained in water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of microwave digestion duration on fluorescence intensity and yield
ce:section.ce:para: Microwave digestion duration determines the reaction degree, therefore leads to varied fluorescence intensity. We investigated the effect of microwave digestion duration on the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots. The reaction was carried out for 10–50 min (10 min interval) in water. The results are shown in Figure S3c. The highest fluorescence intensity was achieved at 30 min duration conditions. The fluorescence intensities at 10 min and 20 min duration conditions varied little, lower than that at 30 min duration conditions, probably due to unfinished reaction and fewer formed luminophores. When the duration time prolonged to 40 min and 50 min, the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly comparing to 30 min. The highest yield was achieved at 30 min duration condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of reaction temperature on fluorescence intensity and yield
ce:section.ce:para: Reaction temperature determines the reaction process of carbon dots. We investigated the effect of different reaction temperatures (140-220℃, 20℃ interval) on the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots. The results are shown in Figure S3d. At 160℃, the highest fluorescence intensity and highest yields were achieved, under which R-CDs was prepared at 160℃. We also noticed that the emission peaks showed slight blue-shift tendency at higher temperatures.

Fig. 2 a is the TEM image of R-CDs. R-CDs is well dispersed with uniform spherical morphology. The particle size distribution is in the range of 2–11 nm. The average diameter was approximately 5 nm (n = 100) (Fig. 1a inset). FT-IR provides functional groups on the surface of R-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1b, the broad peak from 3384 to 3470 cm−1 represents N-H or O-H [31]. 3076 cm−1 represents the stretching vibration of –COOH [32]. 2730 cm−1, 2867 cm−1 and 2918 cm−1 indicate the presence of C-H [33]. 1884 cm−1 proves that R-CDs exhibit stretching vibration of C = O [34]. 1621 cm−1 reflects the stretching vibration of C = N [35]. 1349 cm−1 and 1109 cm−1 represent the bending vibration of C-N and C-H, respectively [36]. XPS is characterized to understand the composition of R-CDs. In the full spectrum of XPS in Fig. 1c, 284.80 eV, 400.63 eV, 532.33 eV represent the main peak of C1s, N1s and O1s respectively. The proportions of C, N, and O elements are 71.90 %, 10.06 %, and 18.04 % respectively. The C1s spectrum of R-CDs (Fig. 1d) was fitted to obtain three peaks at 284.71 eV, 286.10 eV and 287.92 eV, which represent C = C/C-C, C-O/C-N and C = O respectively [32,37,38]. Fig. 1e shows the N1s spectrum, in which the presence of N-H is indicated at 398.78 eV, the peak at 400.70 eV indicates graphite N [39]. In the O1s spectrum, as shown in Fig. 1f, the two fitted peaks at 532.34 eV and 531.04 eV further confirmed the existence of C-O-C/C-OH and C = O [40].

The optical properties of R-CDs were studied using UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 2 shows the UV absorption, excitation and emission spectra of R-CDs. The UV spectrum features two absorption range, 320–370 nm (centered 339 nm) and 566–614 nm. The absorption peak at 339 nm in the UV spectrum represents the n → π* transition of C = O/C = N in R-CDs [41]. In particular, the absorption peaks at 566 nm and 614 nm represent n → π* transitions containing C = O, C = N, C-O and C-N bond [42]. Two emission bands appeared corresponding to the two absorption ranges (“blue emission region” centered at 395 nm under excitation of 339 nm and “red emission region” centered at 625 nm under excitation of 614 nm). The red emission region explains why R-CDs solution exhibited red fluorescence. These features indicate that R-CDs have different emission modes under different excitation wavelengths. The following detection experiments were carried out under excitation of 339 nm. The fluorescence quantum yield was measured to be 16.09 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence spectra and discoloration effect for detecting OFL, GAT, CIP
ce:section.ce:para: The detection performance of antibiotics by R-CDs was studied. First, we examined the detection performance for OFL. New strong emission peak at 506 nm (I506nm) appeared with addition of OFL, while the original emission intensity at 395 nm (I395nm) decreased, as shown in Fig. 3 a. Therefore, R-CDs exhibited detection response towards OFL and a ratiometric fluorescence probe is expected to be developed based on I506nm/I395nm. The solution of R-CDs changed significantly after addition of OFL from red to jade green under 365 nm UV light, which allowed a potential colorimetric analysis platform to be established. Then the detection performance for GAT was examined. New strong emission peak at 493 nm (I493nm) appeared with addition of GAT, while the original emission intensity at 395 nm (I395nm) decreased (Fig. 3b). R-CDs exhibited detection performance towards GTA based on I493nm/I395nm. The solution of R-CDs showed color change after addition of GAT from red to bright green under 365 nm UV light. The third antibiotics to be tested was CIP. New strong emission peak at 501 nm (I501nm) appeared with addition of CIP, while the original emission intensity at 395 nm (I395nm) comparatively decreased (Fig. 3c). R-CDs exhibited detection performance towards CIP based on I501nm/I395nm. The solution of R-CDs showed discoloration effect after addition of CIP from red to light pink under 365 nm UV light. The preliminary results showed that the three antibiotics can be detected by R-CDs through fluorescence spectra and colorimetric phenomenon as well, which were confirmed by experimental results in the following sections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influences of response time, pH on detection performance for OFL, GAT, CIP
ce:section.ce:para: To optimize the detection effect, we examined the influence of response time and pH on performance of OFL, GAT and CIP detection system. As shown in Figure S4a, the sensing behavior was accomplished within 1 min after mixing OFL with R-CDs, indicating a quick sensing for OFL. At the same time, we examined the sensing behavior of R-CDs to OFL in the pH range of 1–13 (Figure S4b). In the range of pH 1 to 9, I506nm/I395nm increased with raising pH and reached highest at pH 9 then dropped when pH was higher than 9. As shown in Figure S4c, 4e, the sensing behavior of R-CDs for GAT and CIP was accomplished in 1 min. Then the sensing performance of R-CDs to GAT in the pH range of 1–13 was examined (Figure S4d), showing that I493nm/I395nm raised before 9 with increasing pH and reached highest at pH 9. Similarly, in the pH range of 1–13, I501nm/I395nm increased from pH 1 to 9 and then dropped from 9 to 13 (Figure S4f). Therefore, 9 is selected as the optimal pH condition for three antibiotics in the following experiments and the detection system was kept for 1 min before testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference study for OFL, GAT, CIP detection
ce:section.ce:para: This fluorescence probe was designed to monitor the antibiotics concentration in human serum. To ensure the feasibility of this fluorescence probe in practical test, some anions and molecules contained in human serum are selected in anti-interference experiment of three antibiotics. For OFL, we first examined the fluorescence spectra of R-CDs + OFL and I506nm/I395nm was recorded. Then anions/molecules were added into the solution of R-CDs + OFL and values of I506nm/I395nm were recorded. As shown in Figure S5a, the anions/molecules have almost no influence on OFL detection performance. Interference experiments were also carried out for GAT and CIP in similar procedure by recording I493nm/I395nm and I501nm/I395nm values respectively. The results showed that these interfering substances have no influence on their detection performance (Figure S5b, c), indicating feasibility of this method in monitoring antibiotics concentration in human serum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection range and detection limit of OFL, GAT, CIP
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the sensitivity of R-CDs in detecting OFL, GAT and CIP, we studied the fluorescence spectra of R-CDs with different concentrations of three antibiotics. First, OFL solutions with different concentrations (0–50 μM) were added into R-CDs and the fluorescence spectra and fluorescence intensity were recorded under optimal detection conditions to set up the linear relationship between I506nm/I395nm and different OFL concentrations. As shown in Fig. 4 a, as the OFL concentration increases, the emission peak at 506 nm intensifies gradually, while emission peak at 395 nm decreases, and the fluorescence color of the mixed solution changes from red to pale bluish green. The linear relationship between I506nm/I395nm and OFL concentration is as follows: Y = 0.0563X + 0.1058, where X denotes the concentration of OFL, Y denotes I506nm/I395nm. The detected concentration ranges for OFL detection are 0.25–30 μM and LOD is calculated to be 27.01 nM (Fig. 4b). The method we constructed can achieve fast and sensitive monitoring concentration of OFL.

Different concentrations of GAT solutions (0–40 μM) were added to R-CDs for sensitivity test for GAT under optimal detection conditions. As shown in Fig. 4c, when the GAT concentration increases, the emission intensity at 493 nm increases gradually, and the fluorescence color of the mixed solution changes from red to bluish green. The concentration of GAT can be detected in the range of 0.25–40 μM quantitatively according to linear equation Y = 0.0821X + 0.1258, where X denotes the concentration of GAT, Y denotes I493nm/I395nm (Fig. 4d). The LOD for GAT detection is 23.73 nM.

For CIP sensitivity test, (0–40 μM) CIP solutions were mixed with R-CDs under optimal detection conditions. As shown in Fig. 4e, as the CIP concentration increases, the emission peak at 501 nm increases gradually and emission peak at 395 nm decreases, and the fluorescence color of the mixed solution changes from red to light pink. The concentration of CIP can be detected quantitatively in the range of 0.75–35 μM according to linear equation Y = 0.0161X + 0.1422, where X denotes the concentration of CIP, Y denotes I501nm/I395nm (Fig. 4f). The LOD for CIP detection is 75.23 nM.

The detected concentration ranges and LOD for OFL, GAT CIP are summarized in Table S1, and compared with the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) range in literatures. The comparison indicates this method are mostly appliable for detection of OFL, GAT and CIP in practice. Better and versatile fluorescence probes for monitoring more antibiotics are to be developed for practical applications. The detection results in this work are compared with those previously reported using CDs or other probes as sensing platforms and summarized in Table S2, Table S3 and Table S4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection mechanism of RCDs to OFL, GAT, and CIP
ce:section.ce:para: We attribute the detection mechanism of R-CDs towards OFL, GAT and CIP to synergistic effects of several aspects. We first speculated the detection mechanism of R-CDs to OFL, GAT and CIP is due to the formation of hydrogen bonds, which is the most accepted theory [43–46]. FT-IR showed that there are hydrophilic groups (hydroxyl and carboxyl groups) on the surface of R-CDs. C-F, C = O, C-N and COOH groups exist on OFL, GAT and CIP. When the quinolone antibiotics are close to R-CDs in the solution, hydrogen bond will be formed. Since R-CDs show different response to OFL, GAT and CIP (different new emission peaks), the subtle and variation of chemical structure and spatial environment of three antibiotics may also account. The electrostatic repulsion between the quinolone antibiotics adsorbed on the surface of R-CDs would increase the distance between the R-CDs particles, which would effectively separate the R-CDs particles from each other, and further reduce the non-radiative decay. Furthermore, the attachment of OFL, GAT and CIP could potentially vary the surface defects of R-CDs, leading to an enhancement of the photoluminescence intensity [47]. Therefore, the detection performance of R-CDs towards OFL, GAT and CIP is the result of synergistic effects.

To exclude the aggregation of R-CDs in the presence of antibiotics, which may lead to new emissions, we measured the fluorescence spectra of a series of R-CDs with increasing concentration which are shown in Figure S6. The results showed that even at high concentrations, R-CDs solutions exhibit emission at 395 nm and no emission appear round 500 nm. So the new emissions at 506 nm (OFL), 493 nm (GAT), 501 nm (CIP) are attributed to the interaction between R-CDs and antibiotics rather than aggregated R-CDs. We can confirm that R-CDs show good stability without aggregation.

We applied this detection method in human serum samples. The results are shown in Table S5. The recovery rates of OFL, GAT, CIP in human serum were in the range of 96.31 ∼ 104.23 %, 97.24 ∼ 105.92 %, 95.36 ∼ 106.42 % respectively, the RSD% for OFL, GAT, CIP were in the range of 1.45 ∼ 4.26 %, 1.59 ∼ 5.58 %, 2.23 ∼ 3.88 %. The results further prove that the method has high accuracy, potential for monitoring antibiotics concentration in human serum samples.

To develop a more convenient detection method, we developed a colorimetric analysis system using test stripe. Under 365 nm ultraviolet light, the color of test strips changed after the serum samples containing antibiotics were dropped on the test strip. As shown in Fig. 5 , the discoloration of the antibiotics on the test strip is basically consistent with that of the aqueous solution. The PS colorimetric analysis system is used to realize visual and more accurate detection, which can determine the drug concentration by recognizing color code, providing a more convenient method for monitoring blood concentrations of therapeutic drugs. The color codes for detection of OFL, LEV, GAT and CIP based on test stripe analysis system are listed in Table 1 , which can be extended to simpler codes (O5-O30 for OFL, G5-G30 for GAT, C5-C30 for CIP). The smartphone-assisted colorimetric analysis system allows adjustment of drug dosages to ensure optimal treatment efficacy, especially for remote hospitals without expensive instruments. Furthermore, the easy-to-operate colorimetric analysis can be carried out by non-professionals without long-lasting special training in regular medication.

In summary, this study developed fluorescence method and colorimetric analysis system for OFL, GAT and CIP concentration determination based on R-CDs. TA and OPD as raw materials are used to synthesize R-CDs using a simple and environmentally friendly microwave-assisted method. The detection range of OFL is 0.25–30 μM and LOD is calculated to be 27.01 nM. GAT can be detected in the range of 0.25–40 μM quantitatively and LOD is 23.73 nM. CIP can be detected quantitatively in the range of 0.75–35 μM with LOD 75.23 nM. The feasibility of the sensing method and analysis system was confirmed in human serum, providing a potential effective and convenient method for therapeutic drug monitoring.

Lei Liu: Writing – original draft, Validation, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Kun Su: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yujie Zhang: Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Qianjun Liu: Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Daohan Zhang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Liang Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by Key Project of Biomedicine Joint Fund of Hebei Natural Science Foundation (B2020208043), S & T Program of Hebei (No. 22567607H), Hebei Research Center of Pharmaceutical and Chemical Engineering (No. 225676121H), Hebei Province Clinical Medical Talents Training Program (303-2022-27-29).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111786.

ce:section.ce:para:
